title_en: Introduction: Ibn Sa'd and the Biographical Dictionary Tradition
title_ar: المقدمة: ابن سعد وتقليد معجم التراجم
content_en:
Kitab al-Tabaqat al-Kabir (The Great Book of Generations) by Muhammad ibn Sa'd (168–230 AH / 784–845 CE) is the foundational biographical dictionary of the Islamic tradition. Ibn Sa'd was the secretary (katib) of the great historian and hadith scholar al-Waqidi (130–207 AH), and his Tabaqat preserves not only his own research but also material gathered by al-Waqidi that would otherwise have been lost. The work provides biographies of the Prophet Muhammad, all of his Companions, and the major Muslim scholars of the first several generations — organized by 'generations' (tabaqat) in roughly chronological order.

The title reflects the organizing principle: a tabaqah (generation) in Islamic biographical literature refers to a group of people who lived and transmitted knowledge in roughly the same era and with comparable proximity to the Prophet. The Companions form the first tabaqah, the Successors (tabi'un) the second, and so on through the major scholars of the second and third Islamic centuries. This generational framework became the standard organizing principle for biographical dictionaries of hadith narrators.

The Significance of the Tabaqat Tradition

The Tabaqat al-Kabir is not merely a reference work but a reflection of the Islamic community's intense interest in the people through whom the Prophetic tradition was transmitted. To know the biography of a narrator — his or her tribe, teachers, students, character, travels, and the circumstances of transmission — was essential for evaluating the reliability of the hadiths associated with their name. The Tabaqat tradition therefore served the hadith critical tradition as its primary biographical source.

Ibn Sa'd's work is particularly valuable because it was compiled at a relatively early date (early third century AH), when many oral traditions about the Companions and Successors were still being transmitted by their students and grandstudents. Later biographical dictionaries had to rely on earlier written sources; Ibn Sa'd had access to living memory as well.

The Biography of the Prophet

The Tabaqat al-Kabir opens with a substantial biography of the Prophet Muhammad, drawing on the sirah tradition of Ibn Ishaq and al-Waqidi. This section covers the Prophet's lineage, birth, the years before prophethood, the first revelation, the Makkan and Madinan periods, the military campaigns, and the Prophet's death. Ibn Sa'd's biography is valuable for the early traditions it preserves, some of which do not appear in the later canonical sirah works.
